publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 165, folios 38-39. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: St. James's Sqre 5 o'clock. Thursdy Afternn. 28th April.

My Dearest William. You are not to be alarmed at seeing pencil instead of ink. I am only a little tired after driving, & think it better to be down, and you know ink & recumbency are quite incompatible. I was very glad of your dear little letter this morning, but am afraid the weather promises little for you.-- I enclose you a hideous little note of Babbages, which I have read.-- Hester

& I have been calling on Madme Tricoupe, who seems to have much affection for you. How very handsome she is, but she speaks no English, so I had to stammer forth French as I best might.-- She has given us some pamphlets containing a funeral address delivered by Monsieur Tricoupe on my father's death.--
I have seen the Philips's today in Mount St. Sir George will enquire about butlers. He said he thought Tubbs would be no loss. Hester seems very doubtful about the capabilities
of Locke's man, though she thinks he is clever & would soon learn his business. I am averse to having a man to teach, & think it would be much better to take one who had already filled the situation.

I have been with Mr. Lauzelle the dentist both yesterday & today, & am to go again on Tuesday. He has tied two of my teeth together as tight as he can, to my great annoyance, & so they are to remain for two or three weeks.
I had a lesson this morning from Miss D'Espourria.--

Hester is a great comfort, & her little room is really very nice.-- She was called for last night to go to a party at Lady Philips's. Your mother & Charlotte come to us on Wednesday Evening. The former sent a very gracious message.
Lord Courtenay called yesterday, & was very friendly.
Your Birdie longs to give ou a kiss, & to peck at ou. Think of it a great deal, & believe it is ou very tender & affectionate little mate, A. A. K.
